pages.text:
UTILITY MASTER COMPILATION
Project: Professor Bray module / The Manufactured Divide — Utility half Status: Working
research compilation, not final output Compiled from: Five research batches conducted in
session Purpose: Single-document reference for the utility-side argument, organized by node
structure, with all evidence, mechanisms, connections, and sources preserved.
HOW TO READ THIS DOCUMENT
Organized in three main sections:
1. NODES — GOVERNMENT, COMPANIES, AMERICAN PEOPLE — each with consolidated
evidence from all five batches
2. CONNECTIONS (EDGES) — directional flows between the three nodes, with documented
examples
3. MECHANISMS — named structural patterns that operate across the node system
Then:
4. QUOTABLE FRAMINGS — short, attributed quotes suitable for direct use
5. WORKING HYPOTHESES — explicitly labeled as such, not elevated to conclusions
6. SOURCES — complete source list with batch provenance
7. GAPS AND PRIORITY CHASES — open threads, ordered by priority
A note on the parallel: per the project's foundational framing, the exaction-utility parallel is
structural, not identity. Breaks in the parallel are as informative as similarities. Nothing in this
document should be smoothed to force equivalence with the exaction half.
PART I — NODES
NODE: GOVERNMENT
Federal layer
Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC)
Exclusive federal authority to set interstate transmission and wholesale market revenue
requirements

In 2022, FERC ruled that two ROE models used in state rate cases ("risk premium" and
"expected earnings") "defy general financial logic" and banned them from federal
proceedings. State PUCs continue to use them.
Applies "special scrutiny" to contracts between utilities and power plants owned by the
same corporate parent (Cross-Subsidization Restrictions on Affiliate Transactions, 18 C.F.R.
pt. 35). Concern per the rule: affiliated sales "could be made at a rate that is too high, which
would give an undue profit to the affiliated [power plant] at the expense of the franchised
public utility's captive customers."
Conducts random audits of utilities. The 2022 FirstEnergy audit (Docket FA19-1-000) is the
single most important federal finding in the project — documented that FirstEnergy hid
HB6-related lobbying/bribery expenses in its rate base, so ratepayers financed the bribery.
Report concluded: "Rate cases did not detect this deception. Only an audit, informed by an
extensive federal sting operation, revealed the utility's deceit."
The 2024 FERC Report on Enforcement (Docket AD07-13-018) summarized multiple audit
findings of improper items recovered in rates: "merger-related costs; lobbying, charitable
donation, membership dues, and employment discrimination settlement costs; improper
labor overhead capitalization rates."
Federal statutory / regulatory foundation
Public Utility Holding Company Act (PUHCA) of 1935 — first major federal utility
regulation
Rural Electrification Act of 1936 — federal loan program extending electricity to rural areas
(only 10% of rural homes had electricity in mid-1930s)
Federal tax equity rules block full solar PPA buyouts in the first 5–6 years (captive-by-deed
mechanism for PPAs)
501(c)(4) dark-money rules are the legal structure enabling utility bribery schemes; no
disclosure requirement
Federal subsidy layer (Heritage/PRI data)
DOE Advanced Technology Vehicles Manufacturing program: $1B+ loans to Nissan and
Ford
Federal loan guarantee program: loans to "giant tech firms, massive energy utilities, large
investment banks, and other successful corporations"
Federal EV tax credit ($7,500): Pacific Research Institute found >99% of EV subsidies go to
households earning $50K+; ~75% to households earning $100K+

State layer — Public Utility Commissions (PUCs)
Structural role
Approve utility rate increases through "rate case" proceedings
Authorize guaranteed return on rate base, typically 9–10% (2024 national average: 9.6% per
S&P Global)
Define what qualifies as capital expenditure (CapEx, enters rate base, earns return) vs.
operating expenditure (OpEx, passed through dollar-for-dollar, earns nothing)
Grant monopoly service-territory exclusivity
Enforce the "cost causation" doctrine — rates should align with a utility's actual costs of
serving a specific consumer
Documented vulnerabilities (Martin & Peskoe 2025, Harvard ELI)
1. Cost-allocation methodologies are contested and subjective. Bonbright (1961): "notorious
disagreements among the experts as to the choice of the most rational method of cost
allocation — a disagreement which seems to defy resolution because of the absence of any
objective standard of rationality."
2. Political pressure on commissioners. Ten states have elected PUC commissioners; the rest
are governor-appointed. Elected commissioners may favor residential ratepayers (voters);
appointed commissioners may align with governor's economic development agenda.
3. Information asymmetry. The utility files its own accounting records, voluminous and
internal. PUCs rely on "the utility's good faith." Recent audits show this trust misplaced.
The consulting pipeline (SURFA)
Society of Utility and Regulatory Financial Analysts — industry-created certification body
Certifies analysts who give testimony to PUCs on rate-of-return
Consequence: analysts on both sides of PUC rate cases (utility and ratepayer advocate) are
trained in the same methods and use the same models
Concentration: 4 consulting firms = 90% of testimony across 60 proceedings reviewed;
more than half of cases testimony from just two people
PUCs "throw out" testimony using non-SURFA methodologies
Four ROE models in use; two were federally banned by FERC in 2022 but remain in state
use
Source: American Economic Liberties Project report by Mark Ellis (Jan 2025)
Specific audit findings — ratepayer-funded political activity

FERC/FirstEnergy (2022): hidden HB6 lobbying/bribery expenses in rate base
California PUC/SoCalGas Decision 24-12-074 (Dec 2024): "a pattern of misclassification of
costs at Sempra Utilities, where the company has charged ratepayers for lobbying, political
activities, and expenses related to outside legal firms"
California PUC/SoCalGas Decision 22-04-034 (Apr 2022): SoCalGas "appears on the face of
the record to have misled staff about the direction of its lobbying"
State legislative layer — naming the extremes
Mississippi Code § 77-3-271(3) (statutory endpoint of the captive pattern)
Strips utility regulators of any authority to review contracts between a utility and a data
center
Contracts not "subject to alteration or other modification or cancelation by the commission,
for the entire term of the agreement"
The captive customer denied even the fig-leaf regulator they nominally had
Kansas Statute Annotated § 66-101i
Explicitly permits below-cost special contracts based on "state's best interest" including
economic development, local employment, tax revenues
Ohio HB6 (2019, bailout-through-ratepayers scheme)
Total $1.5B bailout; larger when OVEC is properly counted (Stokes estimate: $1.7B OVEC
coal portion alone)
$150M/yr 2021–2027 to FirstEnergy/Energy Harbor's Perry and Davis-Besse nuclear plants
Funds to W.H. Sammis coal plant and OVEC coal plants
Total ratepayer surcharge: $170M/yr until 2027 on all Ohio electric bills (residential,
commercial, industrial)
Gutted Ohio's decade-old renewable mandate and weakened efficiency standards
(scheduled post-2026)
Statute written so FirstEnergy would never have to open its books to legislators; amounts
"fluctuated continuously"
Passed Ohio House 51–38 despite widespread bipartisan opposition
Parallel state schemes

West Virginia: $12.5M tax break to FirstEnergy's Pleasants Power Station, in a "hasty
special session" summer 2019. Gov. Jim Justice received $21,050 from FirstEnergy
PAC/associates in 2019 after passage. Justice family's coal company was in civil litigation
with FirstEnergy Solutions over a $3.1M stockpile dispute.
Indiana HB1414 (March 2020): artificial pause on coal-fired plant retirements
Wyoming: uses the same 501(c)(4) dark-money structure to lobby against out-of-state coal
plant closures (NPR investigation)
State-level reform counter-examples
Kentucky PSC: self-imposed guidelines requiring specific findings before approving
special contracts. Discounts allowed only when utility has excess generation capacity;
limited to 5 years and no more than half the contract term; contract rate must exceed
marginal costs. PSC recently rejected a crypto-speculator special contract under these
guidelines.
Virginia HB 2101 (Jan 2025): would require regulators to determine whether cost allocation
methods "unreasonably subsidize" data centers.
State executive / judicial layer — named corruption actors
Larry Householder (Ohio House Speaker, HB6 architect)
Arrested July 2020 on federal racketeering charges
FBI affidavit: 82 pages, 250 paragraphs
$61M in bribes from FirstEnergy routed through Generation Now, a 501(c)(4) controlled by
Householder, his political affiliates, AND FirstEnergy lobbyists
Pre-installation: FirstEnergy funneled millions to 21 candidates who pledged to support
Householder's rise; all 21 supported his Speakership; only 1 voted against the bailout
Had been under federal investigation for money laundering during his earlier Speaker stint
in the early 2000s
Specific personal enrichment (from affidavit):
$300K+ to settle a lawsuit against him
$100K+ on Florida vacation home
$97K direct campaign
Flight to Trump inauguration on FirstEnergy corporate jet
30 phone calls with FirstEnergy CEO Chuck Jones in the six months before HB6 passage
Alleged participants used "pay to play" to describe Householder's operation

Described FirstEnergy funding as "unlimited"
After HB6 passage: FirstEnergy wired additional $38M to Generation Now to block the
Ohio ballot initiative to overturn HB6. Funds paid for:
Ads falsely claiming Chinese government would use signers' personal data
Bribery, harassment, and physical assault of signature collectors
Result: initiative failed; HB6 remains law
Gov. Mike DeWine (Ohio)
Took FirstEnergy money
Staff planned taxpayer-funded plane to fly legislators back for Householder vote
Attended Trump fundraiser hosted by coal baron Bob Murray the day after signing the law
Initially refused to call for HB6 repeal; reversed July 23, 2020 — one day after Stokes' Vox
article published
Regulator-election capture (Arizona)
FBI investigated ties between Arizona Public Service (APS) and Arizona Corporation
Commission Chair Gary Pierce
Pierce met privately with then-APS CEO Don Brandt "numerous times"
APS funneled $700K+ through a dark money group to Pierce's son's Secretary of State bid
APS "secretly spent tens of millions on campaigns to elect its own regulators"
2018: APS spent $40M+ to block a clean energy ballot initiative
New CEO Jeff Guldner played key role in directing dark political spending
Structural implication: utilities financing PUC commissioner elections defeats the
adversarial premise of PUC regulation entirely
State-level counter-example (capture is reversible)
Virginia — Dominion Energy
Before 2017: "atrocious track record of ripping off customers, polluting the climate, and
perpetuating environmental racism" (Stokes)
2017 grassroots campaign pressured candidates to refuse Dominion money
2019 election: ~50 Virginia state candidates refusing Dominion money won seats
Shortly after: Virginia passed landmark 100% clean electricity law
Per Stokes: "Once the Virginia politicians stopped taking money from the electric utility,
they could finally pass climate legislation."

Industry trade architecture
Edison Electric Institute (EEI)
National investor-owned utility trade association
Gave FirstEnergy an award for its work to pass HB6
Stokes' framing: "the industry celebrates it"
Industry tracks and rewards political capture operations — the practice is institutionally
normalized, not treated as scandal
NODE: COMPANIES
Market structure (EIA 2017 data)
~3,000 electric distribution utilities in the U.S.
Three ownership types:
Investor-owned utilities (IOUs): 168 IOUs, average 654,600 customers each, serve
~72% of U.S. electricity customers
Publicly owned utilities (POUs): 1,958 utilities, average 12,100 customers each
Cooperatives: 812 co-ops, average 24,500 customers each, located in 47 states
Largest IOUs: PG&E (5.48M customers), Southern California Edison (5.07M)
Largest POUs: Puerto Rico Electric Power Authority / PREPA (1.47M), LADWP (1.43M)
Largest co-op: Pedernales Electric Co-op, Johnson City, TX (333,809)
Rate comparison (Mark Ellis / AELP, Feb 2025)
IOU residential rates rose 49% above inflation over past 3 years
POU rates rose 44% below inflation in the same period
Ellis estimate: $50B/yr in excess costs to IOU customers; ~$300/household/yr
How IOUs earn profit — the core mechanism
Cost-of-service / rate-of-return regulation
Guaranteed return on rate base every year, for the life of each capitalized asset
Zero return on OpEx (labor, fuel, maintenance, vegetation management, repairs)
2024 national average authorized ROE: 9.6% (S&P Global)

Typical range: 5–10%
$1B in new transmission × 9.6% = $96M annual profit (before taxes)
Earnings trend
2014–2024: American electric utility earnings more than doubled
Growth >15%/yr for the last three years as of March 2024
Morningstar 2023: "best utilities growth environment in decades" — runway tied to
2040/2050 clean energy goals
ROE vs. market returns (Mark Ellis)
Broad market long-term return: 6–7%/yr
IOU actual returns H1 2023: 9.6% — 30% above total market
Low-risk industry should earn less than market average
The Averch-Johnson Effect ("Gold-Plating" / "Green-Plating")
1962 Averch and Johnson paper, American Economic Review (JSTOR 1812181)
Theory: cost-of-service regulation induces regulated firms to pursue non-productive,
capital-intensive projects to maximize profits
"Gold-plating": overspending because overspending pays
"Green-plating" (Energy Bad Boys coinage 2024): current-era version using wind, solar, gas
backup
Maintenance is worse than neutral — maintaining a pole so it lasts 60 years instead of 40
delays capital replacement that would add to rate base
Named example of real-world consequence: PG&E Caribou-Palermo line → Camp Fire.
Inspection was OpEx. A new line would have been CapEx.
Green-plating quantitative example:
Retiring 10 MW depreciated coal plant → 10 MW wind + 10 MW solar + 10 MW gas backup =
triple installed capacity, $44.17M new CapEx, all new undepreciated assets in rate base
Named IOUs with documented profit growth tied to renewable buildout
Xcel Energy (MN): Pre-NGEA (2001–2007) profits grew 2.9%/yr. Post-NGEA profits grew
115% total, averaging 7.6%/yr. 2001 rates 16% below national avg → 2022 rates 7% above.
We Energies (WI): Post-2004 profits grew 94% averaging ~5%/yr. Former CEO Gale
Klappa called "Wizard of Wisconsin" — profit growth exceeded Berkshire Hathaway. 2001

rates 10% below national avg → 2022 rates 7% above.
Arizona Public Service: Profits flat 2001–2009 (-5%). Post-2009 profits up ~100%
averaging 7%/yr.
DTE Electric (MI): 2014–2024 profits +162% averaging 18%/yr. SEC filings cite
"environmental regulations" as part of long-term earnings strategy.
Data-center demand projections (and reasons to doubt)
Industry forecasts (escalating)
Jan 2024: 16 GW of new data center demand by 2030
End 2024: 65 GW by 2030
By 2030, data centers may consume 12% of all U.S. electricity
Georgia Power: total energy sales nearly double by early 2030s
AEP: at least 15 GW of new load by 2030; AEP Ohio says customers interested in 30 GW
Indiana Michigan Power: peak demand 2,800 MW → 7,000 MW
Oncor (Texas): 82 GW potential — equivalent to Texas's entire 2024 maximum demand
Why Martin & Peskoe are skeptical
Utilities have incentive to inflate: "announcements are designed in part to grab investors'
attention"
Double-counting: data centers negotiate with multiple utilities simultaneously; MISO VP
testified "these data centers are showing up in multiple places"
Utilities refuse facility-specific disclosure, citing proprietary information
Technological uncertainty: DeepSeek's efficient approach showed AI energy needs may be
lower than assumed
Scale of commitments
Amazon: $10B Ohio data center (Dec 2024); Mississippi Amazon data center $16B (60%
higher than announced)
Meta: $10B Louisiana data center (Dec 2024)
Microsoft: $80B globally fiscal 2025, $40B+ U.S.
OpenAI: $100B immediate + possible additional $400B (Jan 2025)
Indiana Michigan Power internal estimate: $17.6B new generation to serve 4.4 GW of
hyperscaler load

Named companies — the FirstEnergy / Exelon / APS cluster
FirstEnergy Corp
10 power companies, 6M customers across OH, WV, PA, MD, NJ
Identified as "Company A" in the federal criminal complaint
Went bankrupt 2018 before HB6 effort
Had unsuccessfully lobbied Trump administration for coal/nuclear bailout jointly with coal
magnate Bob Murray
FERC 2022 audit: hidden HB6 lobbying expenses in rate base
1990s: key funder of climate denial
Energy Harbor (former FirstEnergy Solutions)
Emerged from bankruptcy February 2020
Largest HB6 beneficiary
Two OH nuclear plants (Perry, Davis-Besse), one PA nuclear, two coal (W.H. Sammis OH;
Pleasants WV)
Used nuclear bailout funds to save Sammis coal plant (cross-asset fungibility)
ComEd / Exelon (Illinois)
July 2020 (same month as Householder arrest): ComEd admitted to engaging in bribery
$200M fine
Illinois House Speaker Michael Madigan implicated
Parent company Exelon
Arizona Public Service (APS)
2018: $40M+ to block clean energy ballot initiative
$700K+ through dark money group to Gary Pierce's son's Secretary of State bid
"Tens of millions" on campaigns to elect its own PUC commissioners
1990s: key funder of climate denial alongside FirstEnergy
Dominion Energy (Virginia) — the counterexample (see Government node)
Duke Energy — primary documentary evidence
Duke Energy Carolinas v. NTE Carolinas II, 111 F.4th 337 (4th Cir. 2024)
Internal Duke documents disclosed through antitrust litigation revealed:

Offered a larger customer a $325M discount vs. existing Duke pricing
Utility "developed a plan to shift the cost of the discount" to other ratepayers by
raising their rates
Scheme discovered only because a competing power plant developer sued Duke in federal
court under antitrust law — not through rate cases
SoCalGas (Sempra) — see Government node for California PUC findings. Same pattern as
FirstEnergy: political spending hidden in rates.
Solar PPA providers (captive-by-deed mechanism)
Named example: Sunrun
Contracts tied to home title, not homeowner
15–25 year terms, 2–5% annual escalators
Minimal buyout incentive — providers prefer revenue stream
Transfer fees $500–$2,000 at sale
Federal tax equity rules block full buyout in first 5–6 years
Structural point: same captive-geography mechanism as traditional utility, different legal
instrument. Traditional utility: captive by service territory. PPA: captive by deed.
Hyperscale / Big Tech data center actors
Amazon, Google, Meta, Microsoft named in specific regulatory proceedings
Constellation (largest U.S. nuclear owner) and Exelon (largest U.S. delivery-only utility) —
co-location conflict at the center of 2024–2025 FERC proceedings
Until 2022: Constellation and Exelon under same corporate parent. Restructuring into
separate gen/delivery companies set up current conflict.
Industry trade architecture (see Government node for EEI)
NODE: AMERICAN PEOPLE
Captive-geography principle (the project's central claim about ratepayers)
Direct quote, Stokes 2020:
"Since these are monopoly utilities we're talking about, if folks want to stop funding
FirstEnergy's corrupt activities by simply paying the monthly electricity bills, they can't.
There is no choice."

By service territory (traditional utility) — you pay PG&E because you live in their zone
By deed (solar PPA) — you pay Sunrun because the contract is recorded against your
property
Scale of extraction
IOUs serve ~72% of U.S. electricity customers
Mark Ellis / AELP estimate: $50B/yr excess cost from SURFA rate-case capture;
~$300/household/yr
Ohio HB6: $170M/yr in surcharges through 2027 on every electric bill
West Virginia: $12.5M state tax revenue diverted to a single coal plant
Specific cost-shifting burdens
Transmission cost allocation (Martin & Peskoe 2025)
PJM approved $5B in December 2023 transmission projects citing "unprecedented data
center load growth, primarily in Virginia"
FERC allocates regionally, then each state PUC allocates internally
Residential allocation percentages for transmission cost tied to data-center expansion:
Delmarva Power & Light: 68% residential
Potomac Electric Power: 53% residential
Baltimore Gas & Electric: 78% residential
Dominion (Virginia): 50%+ residential
Maryland IOUs average 66% residential
Residential ratepayers did not cause the need for the transmission expansion; data centers
did. The allocation formula routes the costs to residents anyway.
Co-location cost shift (Exelon's own estimate, FERC Docket ER24-2172)
Single proposed co-location arrangement: $58M–$140M shifted onto other ratepayers
Virginia $23M data-center project case (July 2024)
PUC staff finding: the project "likely, if not certainly, would not be needed at this time" but
for the data center
Costs routed through FERC transmission tariff; ~50% to residential ratepayers

Data centers can install backup generators specifically to cover peak hours, avoiding
demand charges
Amazon Minnesota: proposing 600 MW of on-site diesel generators
Cost shifted to ratepayers who cannot predict/respond to the peak
Downstream harms beyond the bill
Deferred-maintenance consequences (Averch-Johnson / green-plating real-world impact)
PG&E Caribou-Palermo line → Camp Fire (named in sources)
The incentive to build new suppresses the incentive to maintain existing
Industrial casualties
Northern Foundry (Hibbing, MN) closed due to Minnesota Power rate hikes; 91 laid off.
Industrial rates +62% 2009–2023.
Wealth-distribution pattern (Heritage / PRI / Master Resource data)
EV subsidies: >99% to households earning $50K+; ~75% to $100K+
Rooftop solar net-metering (39 states): solar-installing homeowners get retail-rate credits
(~14¢/kWh), more than double the ~5¢/kWh paid to conventional power plants. Apartment
residents and non-solar homeowners subsidize the difference through their bills.
Wind turbine lease income up to $8,000/turbine/yr to landowners
Tesla earned $700M from ZEV credit sales under CA mandate
Democratic sabotage — direct attacks on counterweight mechanisms
Ohio ballot initiative to overturn HB6
FirstEnergy wired $38M to Generation Now after passage specifically to block referendum
Funds paid for:
Ads falsely claiming Chinese government would use signers' personal data
Bribery of signature collectors
Harassment of signature collectors
Physical assault of signature collectors
Initiative failed from signature shortfall; HB6 remains law

APS spent $40M+ to successfully block a clean energy ballot initiative
The Stokes "no choice" framing — captive monopolies cannot be boycotted
The user's working frame, stated cleanly by a utility-policy academic: the captive consumer has
no exit. They cannot boycott. They cannot vote with their wallet. The only way to stop funding a
corrupt utility is to stop consuming electricity, which is infeasible. This differentiates utility
extraction from nearly every other consumer-side extraction pattern.
PART II — CONNECTIONS (EDGES)
GOVERNMENT ↔ COMPANIES
GOVERNMENT → COMPANIES (downward flow — authority and rents)
Regulatory authorizations
PUCs grant monopoly service-territory exclusivity
PUCs authorize rate of return (typically 5–10%, 9.6% national avg 2024)
PUCs define rate base (what counts as CapEx, enters the earnings-generating asset pool)
PUCs approve riders/surcharges for specific programs
PUCs grant confidential treatment of data-center special contracts, pre-empting challenge
Direct bailouts and tax breaks
Ohio HB6: $1.5B+ total bailout to FirstEnergy/Energy Harbor; $1.7B OVEC coal portion
(Stokes)
West Virginia Pleasants Power Station: $12.5M tax break
Indiana HB1414: artificial pause on coal plant retirements
Virginia data center sales tax exemption: ~$1B in 2023 alone
Mississippi Amazon package: $300M workforce + 3.15% construction reimbursement +
$500M+ tax exemption
Federal loan guarantees and tax credits
DOE Advanced Technology Vehicles Manufacturing: $1B+ to Nissan/Ford
Federal loan guarantee program: broadly to "massive energy utilities"
EV tax credit: distributes upward to high-income households

ZEV mandate / Tesla: $700M in credit sales alone
Statutory disarmament
Mississippi § 77-3-271(3) strips PUCs of authority to review utility-data-center contracts
Kansas § 66-101i explicitly permits below-cost contracts
COMPANIES → GOVERNMENT (upward flow — capture investment)
Direct bribery (criminal)
FirstEnergy → Generation Now → Householder: $61M pre-passage + $38M referendum
blocking = $99M total
ComEd/Exelon → Illinois $200M fine, House Speaker Madigan implicated
APS → Gary Pierce's son's campaign: $700K+
Pre-installation of allies
FirstEnergy funded 21 Ohio candidates who pledged to support Householder. All supported
his Speakership; only 1 voted against HB6. Distinct mechanism from direct bribery: looks
like normal campaign spending; only visible as capture in aggregate end-state.
Regulator-election capture
APS spent "tens of millions" on campaigns to elect its own Arizona Corporation
Commissioners
New APS CEO Jeff Guldner played key role in directing dark political spending
Defeats the adversarial premise of PUC regulation entirely
Rate-case consulting pipeline (SURFA)
Utility-industry-created certification body trains both utility and ratepayer-advocate
analysts
4 firms = 90% of testimony in 60 reviewed rate cases
Non-SURFA testimony "thrown out" by PUCs
Ratepayer-funded lobbying (documented via FERC audit)
FirstEnergy hid HB6 bribery expenses in rate base
SoCalGas patterns of same behavior per CA PUC
2024 FERC Enforcement Report: industry-wide pattern of recovering lobbying, political
activities, merger costs, charitable donations in rates

Rate-case informational advantage
Utility initiates the case, files the records, controls the narrative
"Information might be too voluminous for the PUC or other parties to verify"
"Ultimately, the PUC relies on the utility's good faith"
Reclassification of OpEx to CapEx
"Grid modernization" and "grid hardening" programs recharacterize maintenance activities
as capital improvements that earn a return
Industry trade association architecture
Edison Electric Institute awards FirstEnergy for passing HB6
NARUC (National Association of Regulatory Utility Commissioners) facilitates
commissioner-utility relationships
Affiliated-entity transfer pricing
FERC flagged the mechanism: affiliated-party sales "could be made at a rate that is too
high, which would give an undue profit to the affiliated [power plant] at the expense of the
franchised public utility's captive customers"
FirstEnergy Solutions/Energy Harbor routed nuclear bailout funds to Sammis coal plant —
cross-asset fungibility within the corporate family
COMPANIES ↔ AMERICAN PEOPLE
COMPANIES → AMERICAN PEOPLE (downward flow — service and consequences)
Deliver electricity (universal-service commitment)
Deliver solar generation / PPA service (captive-by-deed)
Deliver consequences of asset-retirement decisions (premature coal retirements; delayed
plant retirements forced open by data-center demand)
Deliver consequences of deferred maintenance (PG&E Camp Fire named)
Deliver bills that bundle:
OpEx at cost
CapEx with authorized return stacked on top
Riders for specific programs
Transmission allocations where residential pays majority

Hidden lobbying/political-activity expenses (documented via audits)
PPA escalators tied to deed
AMERICAN PEOPLE → COMPANIES (upward flow — captive payment)
Monthly bills — cannot opt out (Stokes framing)
PPA holders — cannot sell home free and clear unless transferred/bought out/removed
Non-solar ratepayers subsidize solar-installing neighbors through net-metering differential
(~14¢ vs. ~5¢)
Industrial customers paying inflated rates absorb capital-buildout cost (Northern Foundry
example)
GOVERNMENT ↔ AMERICAN PEOPLE
GOVERNMENT → AMERICAN PEOPLE (downward flow)
Government sets rules under which companies extract from people
Not a direct money flow; effect is mediated through the company layer
Bailouts structured as ratepayer surcharges rather than direct appropriations — specific
design choice that keeps cost off state budgets while passing to captive ratepayers
State plane used for corrupt legislative vote logistics (DeWine / HB6)
Statutory disarmament of ratepayer protection (Mississippi) — the state actively removes
the only regulator the captive customer had
AMERICAN PEOPLE → GOVERNMENT (upward flow)
Tax revenue that then flows through to utility subsidies (state tax breaks, federal loan
guarantees, federal EV tax credits)
In Ohio: direct suppression of referendum-democracy counterweight (FirstEnergy dark
money funded signature-collector assault)
In Arizona: same pattern with 2018 clean energy ballot initiative
The missing direct counterweight
Flag for decision at compilation: whether to explicitly draw a Government → People arrow
representing "sets the rules of the mechanism." It is not a direct money flow, but it is the single
most important causal arrow in the argument. Without government authorization of monopoly
service territories, the captive-geography mechanism cannot exist. The government is the root
author of the extraction mechanism, even though it does not directly collect from the people.

PART III — MECHANISMS
This section catalogs named structural mechanisms documented across the research. These are
the patterns that operate between the nodes and between the batches.
1. Rate-of-Return Capture (the core mechanism)
Sources: Energy at the Edge, EIA, Energy Bad Boys, Mark Ellis/AELP
Utilities earn on capital deployed; nothing on keeping it working
Rational decision rule: prefer CapEx over OpEx whenever substitutable
2024 national avg ROE: 9.6%; IOU actual returns 30% above broad market (6–7%)
Documented since Averch-Johnson 1962 paper; still operating unchanged
Result: build new things, let old things rot
2. The Averch-Johnson / Gold-Plating / Green-Plating Effect
Sources: Averch & Johnson 1962; Energy Bad Boys 2024
Regulated firms over-invest in capital assets because profit is tied to capital deployed
"Gold-plating" — plate building in gold because gold is more costly
"Green-plating" — current-era version using wind, solar, gas backup
10 MW coal → 10 MW wind + 10 MW solar + 10 MW gas = triple capacity, all new
undepreciated assets
3. Maintenance Starvation
Sources: Energy at the Edge, Energy Bad Boys
Maintenance is worse than neutral — extending asset life delays capital replacement that
would add to rate base
PG&E Caribou-Palermo / Camp Fire is the named consequence
Inspection was OpEx. A new line would have been CapEx.
4. Hidden Value Transfer (Kovvali & Macey formal doctrine)
Source: Aneil Kovvali & Joshua C. Macey, "Hidden Value Transfers in Public Utilities," 171 U. Pa.
L. Rev. 2129 (2023); Martin & Peskoe adopt as controlling term

Monopolist utilities increase profits by manipulating rate-setting to funnel revenue from
regulated monopoly to competitive lines of business
Enabled by: subjectivity/complexity of ratemaking; utility control over rate-setting;
confidential treatment of contracts
This is the central academic term for the user's working hypothesis. It has a Penn Law
Review publication.
5. Cost Causation Violation
Source: Martin & Peskoe 2025, Harvard ELI
Legal doctrine: rates should align with utility's actual costs of serving a specific
consumer/class
PUCs systematically fail to enforce it
The user now has a named, statutory-level vocabulary: the legal standard exists; it's just not
enforced
Bonbright (1961): "notorious disagreements... defy resolution because of the absence of any
objective standard of rationality"
6. SURFA Consulting Pipeline Capture
Source: Ellis/AELP via Prospect/Dayen, Feb 2025
Society of Utility and Regulatory Financial Analysts — industry-created certification body
Analysts on both sides (utility and ratepayer advocate) trained in same methods
4 firms = 90% of testimony in 60 reviewed rate cases
Non-SURFA testimony "thrown out" by PUCs
FERC banned two of the four models federally in 2022; state PUCs still use them
7. Bailout-Through-Ratepayers Loop (legislative surcharge mechanism)
Source: Brookings/Galliher; LPM; Stokes; FERC FirstEnergy audit
Mechanically distinct from rate-base capture — bypasses PUCs entirely:
1. Utility owns unprofitable plants cheaper alternatives would displace
2. Utility funds 501(c)(4) dark money group (federally legal, no disclosure)
3. Dark money group finances politicians
4. Politicians pass legislation imposing ratepayer surcharges

5. Ratepayers pay monthly — captive, no line-item disclosure
6. Utility collects money without rate-case scrutiny
Key insight: PUCs are at least nominally adversarial. Legislative surcharges route around them.
The utility goes around the regulator it would otherwise have to convince.
8. Ratepayer-Funded Lobbying (documented via audits)
Source: FERC FirstEnergy Audit Report, Docket FA19-1-000, Feb 2022; CA PUC SoCalGas
decisions
FirstEnergy hid HB6 bribery expenses as legitimate operating costs in rate base
Audit: internal controls "possibly obfuscated or circumvented to conceal or mislead as to
the actual amounts, nature and purpose of the lobbying expenditures"
Ratepayers literally financed the bribery that kept them captive
"Rate cases did not detect this deception"
This connects Batch 4 (Stokes/Ohio corruption) to Batch 2 (SURFA/rate-case capture)
into a single documented revenue loop.
SoCalGas (CA PUC 24-12-074, 22-04-034): same pattern
2024 FERC Enforcement Report: industry-wide
9. Regulator-Election Capture (APS)
Source: Stokes 2020
APS spent "tens of millions" to elect its own PUC commissioners
APS funneled $700K+ through dark money to Pierce's son's campaign
Distinct from both rate-case corruption and legislative bribery
Defeats the adversarial premise of PUC regulation entirely — the "adversary" owes their
seat to the utility
10. Pre-Installation of Legislative Allies
Source: Stokes / FBI affidavit
FirstEnergy funded 21 candidates' campaigns BEFORE bailout effort, to install
Householder as Speaker
Staged, long-cycle operation — not quick-bribe-and-done
Distinct from direct bribery: looks like normal campaign spending to anyone not tracking
the end-state

11. Money Laundering Across Asset Classes within Corporate Family
Source: Stokes 2020
FirstEnergy Solutions/Energy Harbor used nuclear bailout funds to save Sammis coal plant
Purpose-specific legislative limits don't survive inside a vertically integrated corporate
family
The legislative purpose (nuclear) was disconnected from final disposition of funds (coal)
12. Unauditable Bailout Legislation
Source: Stokes 2020
HB6 was written so FirstEnergy would never have to open its books to legislators
Dollar amounts FirstEnergy claimed it needed "fluctuated continuously"
The corruption is baked into the statute itself, not just in who gets paid
13. Direct Democratic Sabotage (Ballot Initiative Blocking)
Source: Stokes 2020 (Ohio); Stokes via APS case (Arizona)
Ohio: FirstEnergy $38M to block HB6 referendum — dark money ads, bribery, harassment,
physical assault of signature collectors
Arizona 2018: APS spent $40M+ to block clean energy ballot initiative
Pattern beyond lobbying — active suppression of the direct-democracy counterweight
14. Edison Electric Institute as Capture Architecture
Source: Stokes 2020
National utility trade association gave FirstEnergy an award for passing HB6
Industry tracks and rewards capture operations
The practice is institutionally normalized, not treated as scandal
15. Confidential Treatment as Procedural Weapon (Martin & Peskoe)
Source: Martin & Peskoe 2025; 40 state PUC proceedings reviewed
PUCs "reflexively grant utility requests for confidential treatment"
Suppresses third-party challenge before it can start

"Most PUC orders approving special contracts simply conclude that the proposed contract
is reasonable without meaningfully engaging with the proposal"
16. Cross-State Transmission Cost Routing
Source: Martin & Peskoe 2025
FERC allocates regionally; each state PUC allocates internally
Maryland residential ratepayers pay ~66% of transmission costs tied to Virginia data-
center expansion
Data-center-driven costs flow to residential ratepayers in states where data centers aren't
physically located
Specific percentages: Delmarva 68%, Potomac Electric 53%, BG&E 78%, Dominion 50%+
17. Co-Location Cost Shift
Source: Martin & Peskoe 2025; FERC Docket ER24-2172 and 190 FERC ¶ 61,115
Data center connects directly to power plant, bypassing utility transmission
Exelon estimate: single arrangement shifts $58M–$140M onto other ratepayers
PJM Independent Market Monitor: extreme impacts if pattern spreads
Nuclear accounts for 21% of PJM capacity auction clear
18. Peak-Shaving / Demand-Charge Gaming
Source: Martin & Peskoe 2025
Large consumers install backup generators for peak hours to avoid demand charges
Amazon Minnesota: 600 MW of on-site diesel generators
Cost shifted to ratepayers who can't predict and respond to peak
19. Captive-by-Deed Mechanism (Solar PPA)
Source: Solar Panel Exit; Solar to the People; Blue Raven Solar; Solar.com
Contracts tied to home title, not homeowner
15–25 year terms, 2–5% escalators
Transfer fees $500–$2,000 at sale
Federal tax equity rules block buyout in first 5–6 years

Parallel to utility captive-by-territory — same extraction mechanism, different legal
instrument
20. The Duke Antitrust Documentary Proof
Source: Duke Energy Carolinas v. NTE Carolinas II, 111 F.4th 337 (4th Cir. 2024)
Internal Duke documents, disclosed through federal antitrust litigation, explicitly stated
the utility's plan to "shift the cost of the discount" to its other ratepayers
Duke offered a larger customer a $325M discount vs. existing pricing
Scheme discovered only because a competing power plant developer sued under antitrust
law
Rare primary-source documentary proof of the exact mechanism the user has been
describing
21. The Utility-Big Tech Alliance Risk (forward-looking)
Source: Martin & Peskoe 2025
If utilities grow to serve mostly data centers, utilities become financially dependent on Big
Tech customers
Big Tech becomes permanent lobbying force against ratepayer protections
Utilities are "among the largest donors to state elected officials" with "a century of
experience navigating state legislatures and agencies"
Big Tech amplifies this

PART V — WORKING HYPOTHESES
Per the project's foundational framing: working hypotheses remain labeled as such throughout.
Do not elevate to conclusions. Do not cite as established.

WH-1: The utility extraction mechanism is one system, not many
The FirstEnergy/HB6 corruption (Stokes, Batch 4), the SURFA rate-case capture (Ellis/AELP,
Batch 2), the FERC-audited ratepayer-funded lobbying (Batch 5), and the data-center cost-
shifting (Martin & Peskoe, Batch 5) all appear to be aspects of a single revenue loop. Ratepayers
pay bills → utility revenue → political capture → more favorable rates → more ratepayer bills.
Evidence supporting: FERC 2022 audit documented FirstEnergy charging ratepayers for HB6
lobbying/bribery. Duke 4th Circuit documented internal plan to shift discount costs. SoCalGas
California PUC findings of same pattern. 2024 FERC Enforcement Report: industry-wide
pattern.
Not yet settled: the individual mechanisms are documented; whether they function as a unified
system vs. parallel adaptations of the same underlying captive-monopoly pressure is a structural
claim still under investigation.
WH-2: The captive-geography principle is the root mechanism
Both traditional utility rates and solar PPAs use legal instruments (service territory / deed
recording) to lock customers in. Without that lock-in, the rest of the extraction mechanisms
would face normal market pressure. Captive geography is the precondition for every other
mechanism documented.
Evidence supporting: Stokes' direct statement — "There is no choice." Solar PPA sources
documenting title-recording. Martin & Peskoe on IOU monopoly structure.
Not yet settled: whether this framing captures the full mechanism or whether it understates the
role of information asymmetry and procedural capture (confidential treatment, voluminous
records, etc.).
WH-3: Jurisdictional fragmentation shields the pattern from unified scrutiny
The mechanism spans federal tax code (501(c)(4)), campaign finance (state-level), criminal law
(federal, e.g., Householder RICO), utility regulation (state PUCs + FERC), antitrust (federal
district/appellate, e.g., Duke v. NTE Carolinas), and ratemaking (state administrative). No single
court or agency has jurisdiction over the whole pattern.
Evidence supporting: Brookings/Galliher TAP-Plus framing; specific example — FirstEnergy
HB6 corruption went undetected by rate cases, was only caught by FBI + FERC audit. Multiple
parallel state-level schemes (Ohio, Illinois, Arizona, West Virginia) with no federal coordinating
mechanism.
Not yet settled: worth the Jurisdictional Fragmentation panel placement in the final output. The
build-instructions document already has this as a panel. This compilation supports it.
WH-4: The capture is reversible when money flow is cut (Virginia counterexample)

Virginia Dominion → 2017 grassroots pledge campaign → 2019 candidates refusing Dominion
money win ~50 seats → landmark 100% clean electricity law. Stokes' direct framing: politicians
could pass legislation only when they stopped taking the money.
Evidence supporting: Stokes 2020 Vox piece; Virginia legislative record 2017–2019.
Implication: the mechanism is not fixed. It can be disrupted at the Government→Companies flow
(campaign contribution pathway). But the disruption required grassroots pressure, not
institutional self-correction.
WH-5: The exaction-utility parallel is structural, not identity
Per build-instructions and the AI-reader-note in the research doc: the parallel holds on the
pattern of capital-weighted returns, captive geographic customer bases, cost externalization onto
parties without decision-making authority, and jurisdictional fragmentation. It breaks on specific
legal instruments, regulatory regimes, and the role of specific actors.
Evidence supporting: all five batches document the utility half. The research doc and build-
instructions document the exaction half. The parallel is structural.
Not yet settled: specific breaks in the parallel should be named as well as the similarities. This
compilation covers only the utility half.

Priority 4 — Parallel-break examination (for the user's thesis structure)
The parallel between utilities and exactions should be tested on specific breaks. Candidates for
where the parallel may break:

Utility rate-of-return is adversarially reviewed (at least nominally) by PUC; exaction fees
are not reviewed by any adversarial process
Utility revenue requires ongoing delivery of service; exaction revenue is one-time at
permitting
Utility captive-geography is legal (monopoly franchise); exaction cost-transfer captive-
geography is contractual (HOA / deed)
Utility has named institutional architecture (EEI, NARUC, SURFA); exaction-side
institutional architecture is less consolidated or different
These breaks are flagged per build-instructions to be named explicitly, not smoothed over
